Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, honestly I did. I set aside about 22 hours a week for studying, spread across evenings after work and weekends. But with ten online courses running at once, it felt like I was always behind no matter how much time I put in. I'd make schedules, write them down in my notebook, but something always threw me off track. The routine was there in theory, but executing it perfectly was another story entirely.
Q2: I really wanted to study at regular times because that's what works best for me mentally. But the reality was that deadlines from different courses would pile up and suddenly my whole week would shift. Some weeks I'd be consistent, other weeks I'd be cramming at odd hours. With limited resources and no real buffer, any unexpected expense or life issue would derail my schedule completely. I tried my best to stick to a pattern though.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I felt like I had to check if something was due or if there was an announcement I missed. Being enrolled in so many courses made me paranoid about missing deadlines. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I didn't accomplish much. The fear of falling behind was always there, pushing me to check the LMS regularly.
Q4: Yeah, logging in did become automatic after a while. I'd wake up, make coffee, and check the LMS before anything else. It became like checking my phone for messages - just something I did without thinking. The peer support group I was connected with also kept me accountable, so I'd log in to see what others were discussing. It wasn't always productive time, but the habit was definitely there.
Q5: The reading materials and text-based resources were most useful to me since I learn best through reading and writing. I could download PDFs and articles, then annotate them in my own way. The discussion forums were also helpful because seeing how other students interpreted things gave me different perspectives. When peers explained concepts in simpler terms, that often clicked better than the original lectures.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skipped the video lectures or only watched small portions. With over 100 minutes of video content, I just couldn't keep up with all of it across ten courses. Videos take so much time and I can't skim them like I can with text. I also ignored some of the optional supplementary materials because I was just trying to survive the required stuff first.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to what I'd be tested on or what assignments required. If something seemed like it would help me pass, I'd prioritize it. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers because that made me feel less alone in struggling. Anything that felt abstract or disconnected from grades, I honestly couldn't justify spending time on given everything else on my plate.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With required work already overwhelming me, optional stuff got pushed aside almost automatically. There was guilt about not doing them, but I had to triage constantly. Required activities had that urgency attached - miss them and you fail. Optional ones, even if interesting, just couldn't compete for my limited time and energy.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download readings and work through them on my laptop without internet to avoid distractions. I kept handwritten notes in notebooks because writing things out helps me remember better. Sometimes I'd study at the library using their textbooks since I couldn't always afford to buy my own materials. A lot of my learning happened away from any screen the LMS could track.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading printed materials that I'd accessed once online. I'd sit with my notes at the kitchen table, testing myself, rewriting key concepts. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I also studied with a peer support partner in person sometimes, going over material together, but that collaborative learning was completely invisible to the LMS.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main strategy. I'd download everything I could in one session - all the PDFs, lecture slides, reading lists - and then work through them over several days offline. My internet isn't always reliable and I have limited data, so downloading once and studying offline made the most sense. The LMS would show one access, but I might review that material five or six times on my own.
Q12: There were definitely periods where everything felt impossible. When money got tight, my stress would spike even though I usually manage stress okay. I'd be scared about whether I could even continue my education. During those times, even logging in felt overwhelming because I'd see all the things I was behind on. The fear would paralyze me rather than motivate me, and engagement dropped even though I wanted to succeed.
Q13: The workload across ten courses was genuinely crushing. I'd feel fatigue set in and my brain would just stop processing information effectively. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS because seeing it reminded me of what I didn't understand. Competing responsibilities like work and basic life stuff definitely ate into my study time. The LMS became associated with stress even though I kept logging in.
Q14: Many times, honestly. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind would be elsewhere worrying about bills or other courses. I'd watch videos but realize I hadn't absorbed anything and would need to rewatch later. Sometimes I'd click through readings just to mark them as done without really engaging. It was going through the motions because I felt I had to show some activity.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even knowing they were important. The fear of not doing them well enough made starting feel impossible. I'd tell myself I needed more preparation time, but really I was just scared of failing. With low resilience, once I fell behind, catching up felt insurmountable. The assignments I did complete were often rushed at the last minute, which shows in my scores.
Q16: Deadlines were both motivating and terrifying. They'd force me to finally engage with material I'd been avoiding, but by then it was often too late to do quality work. I'd log in frantically as deadlines approached, which probably shows in my activity patterns. The pressure helped me act but not necessarily learn. I wish I could have engaged more steadily instead of in these panicked bursts.
Q17: When I got feedback, it did affect me, but not always positively. Poor feedback made me scared to try again, worried I'd just fail more. Good feedback was encouraging but rare given my performance. I'd sometimes avoid checking feedback because I didn't want to face the disappointment. The peer support helped because getting encouragement from classmates felt safer than official instructor feedback.
Q18: The course structure with so many components across ten courses was honestly overwhelming. Having regular posting requirements in discussions kept me somewhat engaged through the peer interactions. But the sheer volume of content meant I could never feel on top of things. A more streamlined structure with clearer priorities would have helped me focus my limited time better.
Q19: The text-based resources were accessible for my learning style, which helped. But the lack of clear guidance on what was most important made everything feel equally urgent. The discussion forums were good for engagement, but sometimes felt like extra work on top of everything else. Having more structured checkpoints or clearer pathways through the material would have made engagement easier for someone juggling as much as I was.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well actually. I was active in the LMS - logging in, watching videos, participating in discussions - but my actual submissions were low. Fear played a big role; engaging with content felt safer than submitting work that would be judged. Also, with limited resources and time, I could browse and participate but couldn't always complete the deeper work assignments required. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: I can see how that happens for some students who are more efficient or have better external resources. Maybe they have textbooks, study groups, or prior knowledge that lets them succeed without heavy LMS use. For me, it was the opposite - high activity but struggling to convert that into performance. Different students have different circumstances that affect how their engagement translates to outcomes.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when you're active and what you click on. They capture discussion participation and video watching. But they miss the quality of that engagement - whether you're actually learning or just present. They can't see offline study, emotional state, or life circumstances affecting performance. They show behavior but not understanding, presence but not comprehension.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to learn and improve, even when it's hard. It's showing up mentally, not just clicking through screens. For me, engagement included the fear and struggle - I was engaged because I cared about succeeding, even when my performance didn't show it. It's about investment in the process, connecting with peers, and pushing through even when you're scared. My engagement was real even if my results weren't.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was throughout this course, or how much that fear both drove and paralyzed me. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that happened outside the system, the encouragement that kept me going. It can't see my financial stress or how being 28 with limited resources adds pressure younger students might not feel. My engagement was deeply emotional and personal - data can't capture that struggle.
